See /tmp/migration-repair/schema.diff (or re-run pg_dump --schema-only against both DBs) for the full list of remaining differences if you do decide to fix it.

items.title: Federate actor profile updates (display name, avatar, bio)
items.description:
When a user changes their display name, avatar, or bio on one instance, remote instances that have them as a friend (or have otherwise cached them) continue to show the stale values until the 24-hour resolver cache expires. This is one of the first things any real user will notice — federating a friend request but not the display name makes the whole federation story look broken.

Fix:
- On POST /auth/profile (and any other route that mutates actor.displayName, summary, or properties.avatarUrl), enqueue an Update(Actor) activity via the delivery queue to every remote inbox that has a relationship with this actor. Start with the friends set (accepted friendships row with a remote otherActorId) — that's the smallest-possible audience that covers the visible-papercut case.
- Extend federation/inbox.ts handleUpdate to detect when the inner object's `type` is `Person` or `Group` and write the new fields back to the cached `actors` row (displayName, summary, properties.avatarUrl), instead of only handling Note updates.
- Consider tightening the 24-hour STALE_THRESHOLD_MS in resolve.ts for actors we have an active relationship with. For friends, 24h is too long; for one-off message-chain actors it's fine.

Scope-adjacent follow-ups to consider as separate items:
- Group actor metadata updates (server name/icon/description changing on origin → remote followers see the change). Same Update(Actor) shape, different audience (group followers instead of friends).
- Actor deletion — when someone deletes their account, deliver Delete(Actor) to remote peers and have them remove or tombstone the cached row.

items.title: Federate channel lifecycle events (create, rename, topic, delete)
items.description:
After join-remote, the remote instance caches a snapshot of the server's channels as local shadow objects. But new channels created on the origin after the join don't appear on the remote until someone manually re-joins or a refresh mechanism fires. Similarly, channel renames, topic changes, and deletions on the origin don't propagate to the shadow objects.

Fix: when the origin creates/updates/deletes a channel, broadcast a Create/Update/Delete(OrderedCollection) activity to the Group's remote followers. The receiving instance creates/updates/tombstones the shadow object accordingly. Also add a periodic or on-select refresh that re-fetches GET /groups/:slug/channels to catch any activities that were missed (e.g. during downtime).

Small but visible gap — a user on the remote instance clicks a server and sees a stale channel list. Worth fixing before any public federation launch.

items.title: Route federated message edits and deletes through channel context resolution
items.description:
The channel discovery work added context-URI resolution to handleCreate so inbound Notes land in the correct shadow channel. But handleUpdate and handleDelete still operate on Notes by URI without resolving the channel context — so a federated message edit or delete doesn't trigger a WS broadcast to the shadow channel's subscribers, meaning remote viewers don't see edits or deletions in real time.

Fix: after updating/tombstoning the Note in handleUpdate/handleDelete, look up the stored Note's context (shadow channel id), and broadcast a message:updated or message:deleted WS event to that channel's subscribers. The outbound Update/Delete activities already carry the Note URI, so the receiver can find the stored Note and its context. Small change, high visibility — messages appearing to be uneditable/undeletable from the remote side is a confusing UX.

items.title: Cross-instance voice channels via TURN relay
items.description:
Voice channels use P2P WebRTC mesh between participants on the same instance. Cross-instance voice doesn't work because the signaling (offer/answer/ICE) is relayed through the local WS and there's no mechanism for a remote participant's browser to exchange SDP with peers on the origin instance.

Two paths:
1. Forward voice signaling activities through the federation delivery queue so the remote user's browser can establish peer connections with participants on the origin. Requires a shared TURN server that both instances can reach for NAT traversal.
2. SFU approach: the origin runs a media relay (e.g. mediasoup/Janus) and remote participants connect to it directly via WebRTC. More infrastructure but better quality at scale.

Either way this is a significant project — voice federation is a hard problem that even mature platforms (Matrix/Element) have only recently solved. Defer until the basic federation story is stable and there's real demand for cross-instance voice.

items.title: Replace unauthenticated by-slug proxy with HTTP-signed federation requests
items.description:
The message embed proxy (GET /messages/by-slug/:slug) currently allows unauthenticated access for public-channel messages so remote instances can resolve [[msg:slug]] embeds without a session cookie. This works but is too permissive for enterprise deployments that want to lock down their instance — any crawler or unauthorized client can enumerate public message slugs and read content.

The right fix: use HTTP Signatures for the proxy request, same as inbox deliveries. When instance B needs to resolve a slug on instance A, B signs the GET request with the requesting user's (or the instance's) actor key. A verifies the signature, checks that the signing actor is a member of the server that owns the channel, and returns the embed data. Unauthorized or non-member requests get 404 as before.

This requires:
- A signed-GET helper in the federation layer (currently only POST deliveries are signed)
- The by-slug endpoint to accept either session auth OR a valid HTTP Signature from a known actor
- The proxy code in the by-slug fallback to use the signed-GET helper instead of a plain fetch

Small scope, but touches the signature infrastructure which needs careful testing. The current unauthenticated path should be removed once this lands — it's a temporary expedient, not a design choice.

Also worth auditing: the /groups/:slug/channels and /groups/:slug/members endpoints are similarly unauthenticated. Those should also move to signed requests for consistency, though they're less sensitive (channel names and member lists vs message content).

items.title: Federate emoji reactions across instances
items.description:
Reactions (Like activities in AP terms) don't cross instance boundaries. When alice reacts to bob's message on A, bob doesn't see the reaction on B, and vice versa.

Outbound: when a user adds a reaction, deliver a Like(Note) activity to the Group's inbox (for server channels) or to the remote participant's inbox (for DMs). When removed, deliver an Undo(Like). The activity should carry the emoji and the Note URI so the receiver can match it.

Inbound: new handleLike / handleUndoLike in inbox.ts that finds the local Note by URI, upserts/deletes the reaction row, and broadcasts a reaction:add/reaction:remove WS event to the channel's subscribers.

Small scope — reactions are simple single-field objects. Main risk is the N reactions × M followers fanout volume on a busy channel, but the delivery queue handles batching already.

items.title: Federate server metadata updates (name, icon, description)
items.description:
When the origin server's name, logo, or description changes, remote instances that have the Group actor cached continue showing the old values. Same pattern as the actor-profile federation (Update(Actor)) but the audience is Group followers instead of friends.

Fix: when a server admin updates the Group actor's display name, properties.logoUrl, or summary via PUT /servers/:id, enqueue an Update(Actor) to every remote follower's inbox. The receiving instance's handleUpdate already handles Person and Group actor updates and overwrites the cached row.

The missing piece is just the outbound trigger — the inbound handler is already wired from the profile-updates work.

items.title: Federate calendar events (Create, Update, Delete, RSVP)
items.description:
Calendar events on a server are invisible to remote members. When alice creates, edits, or deletes an event on Test Server, bob on instance B has no idea — his calendar view for the remote server is empty.

Scope:
- Outbound: when a server event is created/updated/deleted, deliver a Create/Update/Delete(Event) activity to the Group's remote followers. The Event object carries title, description, startAt, endAt, location, rrule, slug.
- Inbound: new handler for Event-typed objects that creates/updates/deletes a local shadow event row in the events table with the remote server as owner.
- RSVP federation: when bob RSVPs from B, deliver the RSVP to the origin Group's inbox. The origin updates the attendee list and relays the updated counts to other followers.
- The event embed system ([[event:slug]]) should work cross-instance once the event slug is carried in the federated payload, same pattern as message slugs.

Medium scope — events have more fields than messages and the RSVP relay adds a round-trip, but the delivery infrastructure is the same.

items.title: Federate wiki pages (Create, Update, Delete)
items.description:
Wiki pages on a federated server are invisible to remote members. Bob can see channels and messages but the wiki panel for a remote server is empty.

Scope:
- Outbound: when a wiki page is created/updated/deleted, deliver a Create/Update/Delete(Article) activity to the Group's remote followers. The Article object carries title, slug, content, tags.
- Inbound: new handler for Article-typed objects that creates/updates/deletes a local shadow wiki_pages row. The shadow page's server_id points at the cached remote Group actor.
- The wiki translation pipeline should work transparently on shadow pages since it operates on content, not origin.
- Bidirectional links ([[page-slug]]) would resolve locally against the shadow pages. Cross-instance wiki backlinks are a stretch goal.
- Wiki page revisions could be carried as additional metadata on the Update activity, but a simpler v1 just overwrites the shadow page's content on each Update.

Medium-to-large scope. The wiki data model (pages, revisions, links, tags) is richer than messages, and the translation interaction needs testing. But the underlying delivery and shadow-object patterns are the same as channels.

items.title: Server Files — shared file library with translated descriptions and comment threads
items.description:
A per-server file library that acts as a shared drive — upload files, organize them, add descriptions and comments, and reference them anywhere via the embed system. Think a lightweight FTP/SharePoint document library native to the chat platform, with Babelr's translation layer threaded through every text surface.

This should land BEFORE wiki-attachments because it establishes the file-as-first-class-entity model that wiki attachments, calendar event attachments, and message file references all build on. Instead of each surface having its own upload pipeline and orphan-cleanup story, everything points at the same file library, and the embed system ties it together.

Data model:
- server_files table: id, server_id (FK actors), uploader_id (FK actors), filename, content_type, size_bytes, storage_url, slug (10-char for [[file:slug]] embeds), title, description (translatable), tags, folder_path (nullable, for basic hierarchy), created_at, updated_at
- server_file_comments table: id, file_id (FK server_files), author_id (FK actors), content (translatable), created_at. Reuses the same translation pipeline as messages.
- Partial unique index on slug, same pattern as message/event slugs

Server endpoints:
- GET /servers/:id/files — list files, filterable by folder, tags, content_type
- POST /servers/:id/files — upload (multipart), generates slug, extracts metadata
- GET /servers/:id/files/:fileId — single file metadata + comment thread
- PUT /servers/:id/files/:fileId — update title, description, tags, folder
- DELETE /servers/:id/files/:fileId — remove file + storage cleanup
- POST /servers/:id/files/:fileId/comments — add comment to file
- GET /files/by-slug/:slug — embed lookup (same pattern as messages/events)

Embed system integration:
- [[file:slug]] renders an inline file card: icon by content type, filename, size, description preview, download button
- Images render with a thumbnail preview in the card
- PDFs show page count if available
- The file card links to the full file detail view (description, comments, metadata) in the same inline-view pattern as wiki/calendar
- WikiRefKind extended with 'file', parseWikiRefs recognizes the file: prefix
- extractFileSlugs helper for any future link-tracking

Client UI:
- FilesPanel (inline view, same pattern as WikiPanel/EventsPanel): folder tree sidebar, file grid/list, upload dropzone, file detail view with comments
- FileEmbed component: inline card with type icon, name, size, translated description preview, download button
- FileDetailPanel: full metadata, translated description, comment thread (reuses MessageList/MessageInput for comments), version history (if we add it)
- Upload progress with drag-and-drop anywhere in the files view
- Bulk upload support

Translation integration:
- File descriptions flow through useEventTranslation-style hook (content-hash keyed, same cache layer)
- File comments translate via the existing message translation pipeline (they're structurally identical to messages)
- File titles probably don't need translation (they're usually technical names) but the option is there

Permissions:
- New permissions: VIEW_FILES, UPLOAD_FILES, MANAGE_FILES (delete others' files, edit metadata)
- @everyone gets VIEW_FILES + UPLOAD_FILES by default
- MANAGE_FILES is Moderator+ tier
- Per-folder permissions are a stretch goal (defer until there's demand)

Storage:
- Reuses the existing /uploads storage backend
- Per-server storage quota configurable by admins
- Storage metrics in server settings panel

Federation:
- File metadata (title, description, tags, slug) federated via Create/Update/Delete activities to Group followers
- File binary served from origin via the storage URL (same cross-origin pattern as avatars)
- Remote members see the file library via shadow server_files rows, same pattern as shadow channels
- File comments federate as Note objects with context pointing at the file

Why before wiki-attachments:
- Wiki attachments become 'attach a server file to this page' instead of a separate upload-per-page model
- Calendar event attachments become 'attach a server file to this event'
- Message file sharing becomes 'reference a server file via [[file:slug]]'
- One upload, one storage lifecycle, one permission model, multiple surfaces
- The wiki-attachments roadmap item's scope shrinks dramatically: it becomes 'add a file picker to the wiki editor that inserts [[file:slug]] refs' instead of building a whole parallel upload/storage/cleanup pipeline

Shipping order:
1. Data model + CRUD endpoints + slug generation
2. FilesPanel UI with upload, list, detail view
3. FileEmbed component + renderWithEmbeds integration
4. Comment threads on files
5. Translation for descriptions + comments
6. Federation (shadow files on remote instances)
7. Wiki/calendar/message integration (file picker inserts [[file:slug]])

items.title: Project management — second plugin (validates complex SDK case)
items.description:
Second plugin, scheduled after polls + the SDK gap-closure cycle. Validates the SDK at scale: bigger schema, multiple embed kinds, plugin-provided VIEW (first real consumer of the view registry's plugin path), drag-and-drop UI, complex federation.

Scope:
- packages/plugins/project-management/
- Multiple embed kinds: [[task:slug]], possibly [[board:slug]]
- Schema: boards, columns, work items (tasks/bugs/stories/epics), sprints, comments
- Plugin-provided view registered via registerView — kanban panel as a first-class main-panel surface, slot in the sidebar toolbar via the view registry's icon entry (sidebar-render-from-registry should land before this)
- Drag-and-drop column moves, inline status changes, work-item detail with comment thread (reuses useChat against the work-item's chat id, like wiki/event/file comments)
- Federation: work items federate to remote Group followers via AS2 custom objects; remote members can create / update / comment
- Translation: work item titles, descriptions, comments all flow through the standard pipeline. Status labels stay system-defined (system terms shouldn't drift across languages).

Builds on everything polls validated plus pushes on view-plugin-path, federation of custom AS2 objects, and complex UI. After this lands the manifest format is ready to freeze.

items.title: Federate server files to remote instances
items.description:
File metadata (title, description, tags, slug, storageUrl) should federate via Create/Update/Delete activities to Group followers, same pattern as channel messages. Remote members see the file library via shadow server_files rows. File binaries served from origin via storageUrl (cross-origin, same as avatars). File chat comments federate automatically since they use the message pipeline, but the file metadata itself (create/update/delete) needs explicit activity delivery.

Also: the GET /files/by-slug/:slug endpoint needs the same origin-proxy fallback as messages/by-slug for cross-instance [[file:slug]] embeds.

items.title: Per-server storage quotas and usage metrics for files
items.description: Admins should be able to set a storage quota per server (e.g. 5 GB) and see current usage in the server settings panel. Upload should reject with a clear error when the quota would be exceeded. Storage metrics: total files, total size, breakdown by content type. Orphaned file cleanup (storage objects with no server_files row) as a background sweep.

items.title: Drag-and-drop file upload + bulk upload
items.description: The current upload flow uses a hidden file input triggered by a button click. Add drag-and-drop onto the file list area (dropzone with visual feedback), clipboard paste for images (same pattern as message attachments), and multi-file selection for bulk uploads with a progress indicator per file.

items.title: Wiki attachments as server file references
items.description:
Now that server-files exists, wiki-attachments becomes: add a file picker to the wiki editor that inserts [[file:slug]] refs, plus drag-and-drop image paste that auto-uploads to the server file library and inserts the embed. The wiki-attachments roadmap item's original scope (separate upload pipeline, per-page storage, orphan cleanup) is no longer needed — everything points at the shared file library.

Scope:
- File picker button in the wiki edit toolbar that opens a modal showing the server's file library (reuse FilesPanel or a stripped-down variant)
- Selecting a file inserts [[file:slug]] at the cursor
- Drag-and-drop image onto the editor auto-uploads and inserts the embed
- Clipboard paste (Ctrl+V with an image) does the same
- Images from [[file:slug]] embeds render inline in wiki view mode (the FileEmbed already handles this with thumbnails; may want a larger inline render for wiki context)

items.title: Wiki page comment threads via the message pipeline
items.description:
Wiki pages should have comment threads, same pattern as event chat and file comments — each page gets an OrderedCollection on creation, and the comment thread uses useChat + MessageList + MessageInput. Reactions, threads, typing indicators, translation, and federation all come for free.

Scope:
- Add chat_id column to wiki_pages (FK to objects, same as events.event_chat_id and server_files.chat_id)
- Create the OrderedCollection on page creation
- WikiPanel renders the comment thread alongside the page content (below the content or in a side panel)
- Delete cleans up the chat collection (cascade handles messages)
- Migration + backfill for existing pages (create empty collections)

This is a well-proven pattern at this point — events and files both use it. The implementation is mostly copy-paste from the file comments refactor.

items.title: Wiki revision history viewer with restore functionality
items.description:
The wiki_page_revisions table already tracks every edit (revision_number, title, content, edited_by, summary, timestamp) — it's been populated since day one but there's no UI to view or restore revisions.

Scope:
- Revision list view: show all revisions for a page with revision number, editor name, timestamp, and optional summary. Clickable to view that revision's content.
- Diff view: side-by-side or inline diff between any two revisions (or between a revision and the current content). Use a lightweight diff library or compute on the server.
- Restore button: restores a previous revision's content by creating a new revision with the old content (not by deleting history). The restore itself is just an update with content = selectedRevision.content.
- Translation: revision content translates through the existing wiki translation pipeline when viewed.
- Federation: revision history is local to each instance — the origin tracks the canonical history, remote shadows just see the latest content. Restoring a revision on the origin triggers a normal Update(Article) that federates.

Endpoints:
- GET /servers/:serverId/wiki/pages/:slug/revisions — list revisions (already partially exists as revision data is written, just needs the read endpoint)
- GET /servers/:serverId/wiki/pages/:slug/revisions/:revisionNumber — single revision content
- POST /servers/:serverId/wiki/pages/:slug/restore/:revisionNumber — convenience endpoint that updates content to the specified revision

Client:
- Revision list panel (sidebar or dropdown in the wiki view header)
- Revision viewer (read-only render of old content)
- Diff viewer (stretch goal — start with just viewing old versions and restoring)
- 'Restored from revision N' summary auto-populated on restore

This is critical for any real wiki use — the ability to see who changed what and roll back mistakes is what makes a wiki trustworthy for shared documentation.

items.title: Polls & Quizzes — first plugin (validates simple SDK case)
items.description:
First real consumer of the plugin SDK after the runtime ships. Validates schema migrations, ad-hoc DB tables, federation, real-time sync, and translation integration with a small enough surface that finding SDK gaps is cheap.

Scope:
- packages/plugins/polls/ with manifest.ts, server routes, client setup
- Server: polls / poll_options / poll_votes tables; CRUD routes; federation handler
- Client: [[poll:slug]] embed kind
  - renderInline: compact card with bar chart + vote count
  - renderPreview: full poll with click-to-vote, results visualization
  - navigate: ? (probably no full-view target — polls don't need a main panel)
- Real-time vote sync via WS (same pub/sub pattern other live embeds use)
- Translation pipeline integration for poll questions and option labels
- Optional: chat-input '+' menu entry for creating a poll inline (the embed-creation UX)

After polls lands, expect 1-2 SDK gaps to surface (a hook that doesn't exist, a registry that needs extending, federation behavior that surprised us). Fix the SDK; don't paper over with plugin hacks. THAT is the validation cycle — better to find gaps here than halfway through PM.

items.title: Refactor wiki refs to [[wiki:slug]] and design cross-server embed addressing
items.description:
Wiki page refs currently use bare [[page-slug]] syntax (no prefix), while every other embed kind uses [[kind:slug]]. This inconsistency means wiki refs are the fallthrough default in the parser rather than an explicit kind, which will get awkward when the plugin system allows arbitrary kind registration — a plugin that happens to produce slugs that look like wiki page slugs would collide.

Phase 1 — prefix consistency:
- Add 'wiki:' as a recognized prefix in parseWikiRefs, producing kind='page' refs just like the current bare-slug path
- Keep bare [[slug]] working as a backwards-compatible alias for [[wiki:slug]] so existing content doesn't break
- New content created via UI affordances (wiki link picker, copy-reference buttons) should emit [[wiki:slug]] format
- Migration path: existing wiki content with bare [[slug]] refs continues to work indefinitely; no mass rewrite needed

Phase 2 — cross-server embed addressing:
- Extend the ref syntax to support [[server@tower:kind:slug]] for referencing content on a specific remote server
- Example: [[test-server@partner.com:wiki:onboarding]] would resolve to the 'onboarding' wiki page on the 'test-server' Group at babelr-a.local
- The parser extracts the server handle, the embed component resolves it via the federation proxy (signed GET to the origin), and the rendered embed shows the remote content with a visual indicator of which instance it came from
- This turns every embed into a federation-aware cross-reference: a wiki page on one Tower can embed a poll from another Tower, a calendar event from a third, and a file from a fourth — all translating into the reader's language

This is the final piece that makes the [[kind:slug]] fabric truly universal: not just cross-surface, but cross-instance. The addressing syntax is the API surface that plugins and users both interact with, so getting it right before the plugin system ships is important.

items.title: Federation access control — instance allowlists, server visibility, and data sharing policies
items.description:
Currently every instance is open: any remote instance can discover actors via WebFinger, follow Groups, fetch channels/members/wiki/events/files via the AP collection endpoints, and deliver activities to inboxes. Enterprise deployments need granular control over who can interact with their data.

Three layers, each independently configurable:

## Layer 1: Instance-level federation policy

Admin-configured in a new instance settings panel (or via env vars for initial setup).

- **Federation mode**: `open` (default, current behavior), `allowlist` (only listed domains can interact), `closed` (no federation — single-tenant mode for air-gapped deployments)
- **Domain allowlist**: when mode is `allowlist`, a list of domains (e.g. `partner.com`, `vendor.babelr.cloud`) that are permitted to send activities, resolve actors, and fetch collections. All other domains get 403 on inbox POSTs, WebFinger, and collection endpoints.
- **Domain blocklist**: when mode is `open`, a list of domains to reject. Useful for blocking a specific bad actor without switching to full allowlist mode.
- **Auto-accept follows**: whether Group Follow requests from allowed domains are auto-accepted (current behavior) or require admin approval. Default: auto-accept for allowed domains.

Enforcement points:
- WebFinger: reject resource lookups from blocked/unlisted domains (check the requesting IP or require signed requests)
- Inbox POST: verify the signing actor's domain against the policy before processing the activity
- Collection endpoints (/groups/:slug/channels, /members, /wiki/pages, /events, /files): check the requesting domain (via HTTP Signature or referrer) against the policy
- Delivery queue: don't enqueue activities to inboxes on blocked/unlisted domains

## Layer 2: Server-level visibility

Per-server settings, configured by the server admin.

- **Server visibility**: `public` (discoverable via WebFinger and the discover endpoint), `unlisted` (not in discover results but accessible if you have the handle), `private` (only accessible via invite — the server's Group actor returns 404 to non-members)
- **Federation scope**: `all` (federate with any allowed instance), `specific` (only federate with a manually-curated list of partner instances for this specific server). This lets a company have one public community server and one private internal server on the same instance.
- **Content sharing policy**: which content types remote members can access. E.g. allow channel messages but not wiki pages or files. Enforced at the collection endpoint level.

## Layer 3: Data residency and content controls

For regulated industries (healthcare, finance, government).

- **Content classification**: admins can tag channels, wiki pages, or files as 'internal' or 'confidential'. Internal content is excluded from federation delivery entirely — it never leaves the instance, even for members who joined from a remote server.
- **Attachment policy**: whether file binaries are served to remote instances (current: yes, via cross-origin storageUrl) or whether remote members only see metadata with a 'request access' button. Useful when file content is sensitive but the file library's organizational structure isn't.
- **Audit trail**: log every federation interaction (who fetched what from which instance, when). Ties into the audit-logs roadmap item.

## Implementation approach

New tables:
- `federation_policies`: instance-level settings (mode, allowlist, blocklist, auto-accept)
- `server_federation_settings`: per-server overrides (visibility, federation scope, content sharing policy)

Enforcement middleware:
- A Fastify hook that runs before every federation-facing endpoint (inbox routes, collection routes, WebFinger). Checks the requesting domain against the policy. Cached in memory with a short TTL so policy changes take effect quickly.
- The delivery queue checks the target domain against the policy before attempting delivery. Blocked deliveries are silently dropped (not retried).

Admin UI:
- Instance Settings → Federation panel: mode selector, domain allowlist/blocklist editor, auto-accept toggle
- Server Settings → Federation tab: visibility selector, federation scope, content sharing checkboxes

## What this enables

- A hospital runs a closed Babelr instance for internal communication — zero federation, complete data isolation
- A consulting firm runs an allowlist instance that federates only with their three client companies' instances
- A company has a public community server for open-source contributors and a private internal server for employees, both on the same instance
- A defense contractor tags certain channels as 'confidential' — those channels' messages never leave the instance even though the server is federated with partner organizations
- An admin can see exactly which remote instance accessed which wiki page and when

This is the trust infrastructure that makes enterprise buyers comfortable with federation. Without it, federation is a liability ('our data is accessible to anyone who runs a Babelr instance'). With it, federation is a controlled capability that IT can scope, audit, and revoke.

items.title: Babelr MCP server — AI agents as first-class Tower citizens
items.description:
An MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that exposes Babelr's full surface area to AI agents. This is two things at once: a developer tool for using Babelr data in AI-assisted workflows, and an integration layer that makes AI agents first-class participants in Towers.

As a developer tool:
- AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot) can read and write to Babelr directly via MCP
- Query wiki pages for context while coding: 'What does the API spec say about rate limiting?'
- Search messages for decisions: 'When did we decide to switch to PostgreSQL?'
- Create wiki pages from conversation summaries
- Reference Babelr content via [[server@tower:kind:slug]] in commit messages, PR descriptions, and docs — the AI can resolve and inline the content
- File operations: upload build artifacts, read shared design assets
- Calendar awareness: 'What meetings do I have today?' 'Create an event for the sprint review'

As an AI agent integration layer:
- AI agents can be configured as actors on a Tower — they get an actor profile, can join servers, post in channels, respond to mentions, and participate in conversations
- A server admin can add an AI agent to their server that: answers questions from the wiki, summarizes long threads, translates on demand, triages bug reports into the project management board, monitors channels for action items
- Agents interact through the same API surface as human users — they see the same permissions, the same content, the same embed system. An agent can post a [[wiki:summary]] embed just like a human can.
- Agent configuration UI: per-server, admins pick which agent to activate, configure its system prompt, set which channels it monitors, what permissions it has
- The translation pipeline means an agent can be configured in one language and respond in each user's preferred language automatically

MCP server shape:

Resources (read):
- babelr://servers — list servers the agent has access to
- babelr://servers/{id}/channels — channel list
- babelr://servers/{id}/channels/{id}/messages — message history
- babelr://servers/{id}/wiki/pages — wiki page list
- babelr://servers/{id}/wiki/pages/{slug} — single page content
- babelr://servers/{id}/events — calendar events
- babelr://servers/{id}/files — file library
- babelr://dms — DM conversations

Tools (write):
- send_message(channelId, content) — post a message
- create_wiki_page(serverId, title, content) — create a wiki page
- update_wiki_page(serverId, slug, content) — edit a page
- create_event(serverId, title, startAt, endAt, ...) — create a calendar event
- upload_file(serverId, file, metadata) — upload to the file library
- search_messages(query, channelId?) — full-text search
- resolve_embed(server, tower, kind, slug) — resolve a cross-tower embed

Prompts (templates):
- summarize_thread(channelId, messageId) — summarize a thread
- draft_wiki_page(topic, context) — draft a wiki page from context
- translate_content(content, targetLang) — on-demand translation

Authentication:
- The MCP server authenticates as a specific actor on the Tower (API key or session token)
- For developer-tool use: authenticates as the developer's own actor
- For agent use: authenticates as a dedicated agent actor with its own permissions

Package:
- packages/mcp-server/ in the monorepo
- Published as @babelr/mcp-server on npm
- Configurable via claude_desktop_config.json, .cursor/mcp.json, etc.
- Can point at any Tower (local dev, staging, production)

Why this matters:
- Every Tower becomes an AI-accessible knowledge base. The wiki, the message history, the file library, the calendar — all queryable by agents.
- The embed system means AI-generated content seamlessly references human-authored content and vice versa. An agent can write a wiki page that embeds [[msg:slug]] refs to the chat messages it summarized.
- Federation means agents on one Tower can reference content on other Towers via [[server@tower:kind:slug]] — a coding agent can pull in the API spec from the engineering Tower while summarizing a bug report from the support Tower.
- The translation pipeline means a single agent configuration works for multilingual teams without any per-language setup.

items.title: Wiki page hierarchy — parent-child nesting, sidebar tree, breadcrumbs
items.description:
Pages are currently flat — a list sorted by recency with no structural relationship between them. Mature wikis have nested pages (a page can be a child of another page), which gives the wiki a navigable tree structure.

Scope:
- Add parent_id (nullable FK to wiki_pages.id) column to wiki_pages
- Sidebar renders as a collapsible tree instead of a flat list
- Breadcrumb trail shows the page's ancestry (Home > Engineering > API > Rate Limiting)
- Page ordering within a sibling group (position integer, drag-to-reorder)
- Table of contents auto-generated from headings within a page
- 'Move page' action to reparent a page in the tree

Critical for import: Confluence, Notion, BookStack, and Obsidian all have hierarchical page structures. Without parent-child support, imported content loses its organizational structure and becomes a flat soup of pages.

Also needed for the project wiki dogfooding — architectural docs need to nest (Federation > Delivery Queue > Retry Logic).

items.title: Wiki page templates — meeting notes, decision records, RFCs, runbooks
items.description:
No template system exists. When creating a page, the user gets a blank editor. Templates provide starter content for common page types.

Scope:
- Template library per server (stored as wiki_page_templates table or as specially-tagged pages)
- 'New from template' in the create page flow — shows template picker
- Built-in templates shipped with every server: Meeting Notes, Decision Record (ADR), RFC, Runbook, Onboarding Guide
- Custom templates: any page can be saved as a template
- Templates are just markdown content with placeholder tokens ({{date}}, {{author}}, {{title}})
- Templates translate — the template content flows through the translation pipeline so a Japanese user sees the Meeting Notes template in Japanese

Important for adoption — templates reduce the 'blank page' friction that stops people from writing docs.

items.title: Full-text search across wiki page content
items.description:
Currently the sidebar only filters by title/slug substring (client-side). No server-side full-text search of page content exists.

Scope:
- Add a content_search tsvector column to wiki_pages (same pattern as objects.content_search for messages)
- Populate via trigger or on insert/update
- GET /servers/:serverId/wiki/search?q=query endpoint with ranked results
- Search UI in the wiki sidebar with content preview snippets and highlighted matches
- Tag-based filtering in the search API (q=query&tag=architecture)

Note: the message search infrastructure already exists (objects.content_search with GIN index + to_tsquery). Wiki search can reuse the same pattern.

items.title: Wiki content enhancements — syntax highlighting, callouts, task lists, ToC
items.description:
The markdown renderer supports basic formatting but lacks features that make wiki content rich and scannable.

Scope:
- Syntax-highlighted code blocks via highlight.js or Prism (language detection from the fenced block tag)
- Callout/admonition blocks (> [!NOTE], > [!WARNING], > [!TIP] — GitHub-flavored)
- Task lists (- [ ] / - [x] checkboxes, interactive toggle)
- Auto-generated table of contents from headings (insertable via a [[toc]] directive or always-visible sidebar)
- Footnotes
- Mermaid diagram rendering (stretch goal — adds a large dependency)

The syntax highlighting is the most impactful — any engineering wiki without it looks amateur.

items.title: Wiki navigation — recently edited, all pages, orphaned pages, page stats
items.description:
The sidebar shows a flat list with tag filter and search. Missing the discovery features that help users find and maintain content.

Scope:
- 'Recently edited' section showing the last N pages modified (with editor name and timestamp)
- 'All pages' alphabetical index view
- 'Orphaned pages' — pages with no backlinks (nothing links to them), surfaced so admins can either link them or delete them
- Page statistics: view count (requires a page_views table), edit count (already available from revisions), last editor
- 'Related pages' suggestions based on shared tags or backlink graph proximity

items.title: Wiki export — markdown bundle, PDF, HTML
items.description:
No export capability exists. Users need to be able to extract their wiki content in portable formats.

Scope:
- Export single page as markdown (.md file download)
- Export single page as PDF (server-side rendering via Puppeteer or a lightweight HTML-to-PDF library)
- Export entire wiki as a zip of markdown files (preserving folder structure from page hierarchy)
- Export preserves [[wiki:slug]] refs as relative links between files
- Export includes embedded images as local files with rewritten paths

Critical for enterprise: procurement teams ask 'can we get our data out?' and the answer must be yes.

items.title: Generic right-hand sidebar component
items.description: Extract the wiki tabbed right sidebar into a reusable component for all views. Chat could show threads/embed previews, files could show metadata, and plugins could register their own interactive panes via the plugin API.

items.title: Voice federation: optional proxied signaling toggle
items.description:
Default federated voice signaling is direct: the remote user's browser opens a WS straight to the origin Tower (the one that owns the voice channel) using a federation JWT issued by their home Tower. This is simple, low-latency, and lets the origin Tower see the user's browser IP.

Some self-hosters will want privacy-preserving signaling that hides member IPs from remote Towers. Add a config toggle (per-Tower) that enables proxied signaling: the browser only ever talks to its home Tower, which relays every voice WS frame to the origin Tower. The origin Tower sees the home Tower's IP, never the user's.

Scope:
- Per-Tower config: VOICE_FEDERATION_PROXY=on|off (default off)
- Home Tower opens an outbound WS to origin Tower; multiplex per-user voice signaling over it
- Add latency budget instrumentation so admins can see the cost
- The WebRTC media itself still flows browser ↔ origin SFU — proxy mode does NOT relay RTP
  (that would require SFU-to-SFU cascading, see voice-channels-sfu cascading)

Trade-off worth surfacing in docs: with proxied signaling on, an origin Tower can still infer approximate user identity from the home Tower's announce IP if the WebRTC media flows direct. True browser-IP hiding requires both proxied signaling AND SFU-to-SFU cascading.

items.title: Firefox voice/video parity test on staging deployment
items.description:
Firefox fails ICE when joining a voice channel against a mediasoup SFU running on localhost loopback (same-machine dev). Chromium works end-to-end under identical configuration. We tried the usual mitigations (MEDIASOUP_ANNOUNCED_IP=127.0.0.1, UDP-only transports, STUN servers, FF about:config privacy flags including obfuscate_host_addresses and ice.loopback); none helped.

Hypothesis: Firefox's WebRTC stack on Linux rejects loopback/private-network candidates from an ICE-Lite remote more aggressively than Chromium, even with the privacy flags disabled. In production, mediasoup announces a real public IP via MEDIASOUP_ANNOUNCED_IP, so Firefox should accept those candidates normally.

Full writeup: .daedalus/open-issues/firefox-sfu-loopback.md

Test plan (run on first staging deploy with a public IP):
1. FF + FF, 2 participants, audio
2. FF + Chrome, 2 participants, audio
3. FF webcam enable/disable/re-enable (the original voice-video-reenable-bug — should be dead under SFU topology regardless of browser)
4. FF screen-share enable/disable
5. FF 3+ participants

If FF still fails on a real public IP, the issue is in our mediasoup setup, not localhost — open a deeper investigation. Mediasoup's GitHub issues have several FF-specific handler threads worth consulting if we reach that branch.

items.title: Live federated voice end-to-end test (real Tower deployments)
items.description:
Federated voice channels (cross-Tower join via signed JWT handshake) ship in the SFU branch with full unit + integration test coverage of the auth path. The end-to-end browser flow is verified for SAME-TOWER voice on Chromium, but federated voice across two Towers in dev (the dev-two-instance.sh harness) hits a wall of dev-environment issues that don't apply in production:

1. babelr-a.local / babelr-b.local over plain HTTP isn't a 'secure context' in the browser, so crypto.subtle is undefined — blocks E2E key gen before voice can even run. Workaround: chromium --unsafely-treat-insecure-origin-as-secure=...
2. Even with that bypassed, mediasoup ICE candidates and cross-Tower WS dial in dev have several moving pieces (vite proxy port vs API port, etc.) that don't reflect any real deployment shape.

Validation should run against TWO REAL Tower deployments with public DNS, valid TLS, and proper MEDIASOUP_ANNOUNCED_IP. Test matrix:
- Tower B user joins a voice channel on Tower A (must already be a federated member of Tower A's owning Group). Audio flows.
- Same with webcam, then screen-share
- Federation policy on Tower A blocks Tower B's domain → token issuance returns 403
- Tower B user is removed from the Group → next token issuance returns 403
- Token expiry: held WS keeps working (session is bound to the WS, not the JWT TTL); fresh WS attempt past the JWT exp gets 401
- Firefox parity per the firefox-staging-voice-parity-test item

If the dev rig becomes worth fixing later, the right move is: run vite over HTTPS via mkcert, treat babelr-a.local / babelr-b.local as trusted origins, and add the cert chain installation to docs/FEDERATION_TESTING.md. Tracked separately if desired.

items.title: Full-size CallView for active voice channels (Discord-style call surface)
items.description:
When a user is in a voice call AND has the voice channel selected, replace the (empty) message-list panel with a full-size call surface (Discord-style):

- Participant grid when nobody is screen-sharing — responsive auto-fit, square-ish tiles, scales to count
- When someone IS screen-sharing: large 16:9 screen pane on top, horizontal strip of small participant tiles below
- Right sidebar with tabbed UI; default tab is the channel's own text chat (voice channels accept text messages alongside voice). Same useChat / MessageList / MessageInput composition the wiki/event/file panels use.
- Fuller channel header (topic, member count, settings gear) — matches the text-view header for consistency
- Control bar (mute / cam / screen / deafen / leave) extracted into a shared component used by both CallView and the floating VoicePanel

Floating VoicePanel widget still shows when call is active but the user navigated to a different channel; hidden when CallView is the visible surface.

Built generic: `chatId` is the only chat-source prop, so DM calls and event calls can later reuse the same surface by passing dmChatId / eventChatId.

Side benefit: extract the right-sidebar tab pattern (currently inline-duplicated in WikiPanel, EventDetailPanel, FilesPanel) into a reusable RightSidebar component. Don't retrofit existing consumers in this PR — just have them available for future consolidation.

Follow-ups (separate roadmap items):
- Wire CallView for DM calls (needs DM voice gating)
- Wire CallView for event calls (needs event voice gating)
- Retrofit WikiPanel / EventDetailPanel / FilesPanel onto RightSidebar

items.title: Embed sidebar previews + plugin-extensible embed registry
items.description:
Replace the click-to-navigate behavior on inline message embeds with click-to-preview-in-sidebar behavior. Reuses the RightSidebar component from CallView. The previewed content gets a header with a close button + 'Open in [X]' navigation button so the existing full-view destinations (WikiPanel, EventsPanel, FilesPanel, etc.) remain reachable.

The lift that matters: introduce an EmbedRegistry abstraction that maps `kind → { renderInline, renderPreview, navigate, label }`. Today the inline rendering and click navigation are hardcoded across MessageList / ChatView / individual embed components. The registry collapses all that into one extensible table that core kinds populate at app boot. Plugins (via the future embed-plugin-system) register against the same API, so plugin authors get full parity with first-party embed kinds — including their own sidebar preview UI alongside their main view.

Existing kinds covered:
- wiki (full page markdown render in sidebar)
- man (wiki variant — same renderer, different navigate target)
- event (title + description + when + attendees + RSVP)
- file (metadata card + comments preview)
- img (file variant — image-focused preview)
- message (message body + author + small surrounding context)

Cross-server embeds ([[server@tower:kind:slug]]) use the existing federation proxy to fetch, then dispatch to the same renderPreview for the resolved kind.

Sidebar behavior:
- Close on channel switch (sidebar is per-channel context)
- Single-tab for now; future could add tabs for additional context
- Desktop-first layout; responsive narrow-width is a follow-up

Follow-ups (separate items):
- Wire the registry into the actual embed-plugin-system manifest (when that lands)
- Mobile / narrow-width responsive sidebar

items.title: View registry — universal primitive for plugin-extensible main panels
items.description:
Mirror the embed registry pattern for main-panel views. The current mainView switch in ChatView ('chat' | 'calendar' | 'wiki' | 'files' | 'manual') becomes registry-driven: each first-party view registers itself at boot with `{ id, label, icon, isAvailable, render }`, and ChatView's chat-panel slot dispatches to whichever view is active.

Plugin authors then register against the same API to ship their own panels — the project-management plugin, polls dashboard, third-party integrations, etc. all work the same way the wiki/calendar do today.

Scope:
- ViewRegistry types + singleton (registry.ts pattern matching embeds)
- Builtin registrations: calendar, wiki, files, manual
- Chat stays as the always-on default background (not in registry; rendered when no active view)
- ViewHostContext bag bundles cross-cutting primitives (actor, selectedServer, callerRole, channels, navCtx, openEmbedPreview, closeView) so registrations don't need to wire 30 props
- Sidebar entry buttons iterate the registry (server toolbar)
- Per-view deep-link state held generically on ChatView and exposed to render(host, viewState)

Out of scope (separate items):
- CallView stays inline-conditional (it's context-triggered, not entry-point-driven)
- Channel-type plugin support (separate concern from main-panel views)
- Embed-plugin-system manifest format (this just builds the API; manifest + lazy loading is the next item)

items.title: First-run onboarding flow — guided server creation + language preference
items.description:
Today's first-run is 'register, log in, see an empty Discord-shaped UI'. That's fine for builders evaluating the project; it's friction for friend groups, hobbyist communities, and small teams who haven't seen ActivityPub before and don't know what a Tower is.

Add a guided onboarding sequence after first registration:
1. Welcome + 30-second pitch (the connected-workspace thesis, one screen)
2. Language preference picker (was a hidden setting; now in the path)
3. Choose: create your own server, accept an invite link, or browse the manual
4. If create-server: name + tagline + first channel, with sensible defaults pre-filled
5. 'You're done' state with one-click hooks: invite a friend, write your first wiki page, schedule an event

Skippable for power users; persists 'completed onboarding' on the actor profile so it doesn't fire again. Self-host operators may want to customize the welcome message and default-server template — make those Tower-config-driven so admin Towers can present a coherent landing for their own user base.

This is the conversion line between 'beautifully architected platform' and 'something a non-builder will actually adopt'. Without it the rest of the work mostly serves people who would have figured it out anyway.

items.title: PWA support — installable web app + offline shell as a quick mobile path
items.description:
Capacitor (mobile-apps-capacitor) is the proper native mobile path but it's a bigger lift — code-signing, store submission, device APIs, iOS review queue, etc. PWA support is the quick win that gets us most of the way there:

- Web App Manifest (icon, name, theme color, display: standalone)
- Service worker for offline shell + cached assets
- 'Add to Home Screen' prompt on supported browsers
- Push notifications via the Push API (web push / VAPID)

Trade-offs vs Capacitor:
- iOS PWA support is real but limited (no background sync, no push until iOS 16.4+, limited storage). Acceptable for a v1 mobile experience; not acceptable as a final answer.
- Android PWA is excellent and basically reaches feature parity for chat use cases.
- Distribution is easier (no app stores) but discoverability is worse.

Land PWA first as the bridge; ship Capacitor when the native-only features (background audio for voice channels, system-level notifications, file pickers, camera/mic on iOS) matter enough to justify the operational overhead.

items.title: DM voice calls — 1:1 + group DM voice with incoming-call notifications
items.description:
Voice/video/screen-share between DM participants — 1:1 and group DMs both. The CallView we shipped is already generic over chatId, so the rendering side is free; the work is on auth/routing and the call-initiation UX.

Server:
- Extend the SFU room model to accept ad-hoc rooms keyed by DM id rather than channel id. The room ID space becomes 'channel:<id>' | 'dm:<id>' | 'event:<id>' (the last one for the event-calls follow-up).
- Permission check: any participant of the DM (via the dm_participants table) can join.
- New WS message (or reuse voice:join with a room-type hint): 'voice:invite' fans an incoming-call notification to other DM participants.

Client:
- 'Call' button in the DM header (audio-only / video / screen-share variants).
- Incoming-call modal/toast for invitees, with accept/decline. Ringer sound. Auto-decline after a timeout if all invitees are away/offline.
- CallView used as-is, with chatId = the DM id, so the right-sidebar text chat shows the DM history alongside the call.

E2E caveat (must surface in UI): mediasoup SFU sees plaintext RTP. Voice in E2E DMs is NOT end-to-end encrypted today even though messages are. Show a 'voice not E2E' indicator during DM calls. Real fix is later (group MLS already on the roadmap; voice-MLS is a separate research item).

Federation: a DM call between actors on different Towers needs the federated-voice JWT handshake the same way a federated channel call does. The DM's owning Tower (whoever created the DM, or a deterministic rule based on actor URIs) hosts the SFU room; the remote actor's home Tower issues a token via the existing endpoint.

items.title: Event voice calls — built on DM call infrastructure, scoped to attendees
items.description:
Voice/video/screen-share for calendar events — essentially DM calls with a different permission scope. Reuses the SFU ad-hoc room infrastructure from the dm-voice-calls item.

Built on top of dm-voice-calls — implement that first; this is then ~a day of work:

- SFU room keyed as 'event:<id>'
- Permission: anyone who can RSVP to the event can join (effectively: the event's audience). For server-scoped events that's CONNECT_VOICE on the owning Group; for user-scoped events it's the event owner's friends/invitees.
- 'Join voice' button on the event detail panel and the event embed card. Optional auto-popup of an incoming-call notification on event start time for RSVPs marked Going.
- CallView with chatId = the event's eventChatId so the right-sidebar text chat is the event discussion thread (which already exists).
- Recurring events: each occurrence gets its own SFU room scoped by occurrence start time. Don't carry call history across recurrences.

Federation: event calls follow the federated-voice path the same way DM calls and channel calls do. Remote attendees' home Tower issues the JWT; browser dials origin Tower's SFU.

This closes the loop on the connected-workspace pitch — every surface that has a 'people' component (channel, DM, event) supports voice. The wiki and files don't, on purpose: those are knowledge surfaces, not synchronous-presence surfaces.

items.title: Sidebar entries iterate the view registry (auto-toolbar for plugin views)
items.description:
Today the Calendar / Wiki / Files toolbar buttons in ChannelSidebar are hardcoded as individual props (onShowCalendar, onShowWiki, onShowFiles). The view registry knows about all registered views including plugin-provided ones, but plugin views can't show up in the toolbar without a code change to ChatView.

Refactor: ChannelSidebar takes `entries: Array<{ id, label, icon, onClick }>` instead of individual handlers; ChatView builds it from listViews().filter(v => v.icon && (v.isAvailable?.(host) ?? true)).map(v => ({ id: v.id, label: v.label, icon: v.icon, onClick: () => openView(v.id) })). Plugin-provided views automatically get a sidebar button. Should land BEFORE project-management plugin (PM provides a view; without this, the kanban panel is unreachable from the sidebar).

Small refactor; the registry already has the data it needs. Net effect: plugin authors register a view with an icon, it shows up — no host changes.

items.title: Plugin distribution — npm packaging, admin install flow, manifest freeze
items.description:
After polls + project-management validate the manifest format end-to-end, freeze the API and ship the public-facing distribution layer.

Scope:
- npm package conventions: @babelr/plugin-<name>, peer-deps on @babelr/plugin-sdk + @babelr/shared. Document the layout (manifest.ts as entry, server/ + client/ subdirs).
- Tower admin install flow: `npm install @babelr/plugin-foo` → restart Tower → plugin appears in admin UI with enable toggle. Auto-runs migrations on enable.
- Plugin compatibility check: dependencies.babelr semver vs Tower's version. Refuse to load incompatible plugins, surface clearly in admin UI.
- Plugin author guide in the manual: how to write one from scratch, the trust model, the review process for getting on a curated plugin list (if we maintain one).
- Optional: a `babelr plugin scaffold <name>` CLI to generate a starter plugin from a template.

Manifest freeze means: any further changes to the manifest format are additive and backwards-compatible. Plugins built against version N keep working under version N+M. If we need to break, it's a major version bump and we ship a migration path.

items.title: Translation as default for plugin content — zero-config primitive
items.description:
Today polls translates correctly, but only because the plugin wires up useTranslateStrings + useTranslationSettings manually and remembers to map every translatable field through them. That's friction plugin authors won't reliably pay — the default path is 'content renders in the author's language' and the translation path is 'extra code.' Babelr's core promise is multilingual-by-default, so plugin content needs to follow that default too.

Goal: plugin authors write normal JSX with plaintext strings. Translation happens automatically. The opt-out case (system identifiers, code snippets, URLs) is where you write extra code — not the translated case.

Options to evaluate:

1. **`<T>` component** — `<T>{poll.question}</T>` becomes translated at render. Trivial to adopt, trivial to forget. Zero config but requires discipline.

2. **Manifest-declared translatable fields** — plugin declares `translatableFields: ['question', 'options[].label']` in the embed kind definition. SDK/client transparently translates those fields on fetched content before handing to renderInline/renderPreview. Declarative, centralized, but requires plugin authors to enumerate their fields upfront.

3. **Auto-wrap every string child** — a React context wrapper at the plugin-render boundary that proxies all string children through useTranslateStrings. Most magical, most fragile — grabs strings that shouldn't be translated (ids, URLs, technical identifiers) unless the plugin author marks them skip.

4. **Server-side translation in the federation handler / API response** — translate the content server-side before shipping to the client, keyed off the caller's preferredLanguage. Translation decisions happen closer to the data. Higher server load, but plugins don't have to think about translation at all on the client.

Lean toward option 2 (declarative) + option 1 (`<T>` component) as a belt-and-suspenders pair: most content is automatically translated via the declarative field list; edge cases the plugin author knows about get `<T>` wrapping. Option 4 is attractive but worse for live content (poll option labels change via drag-and-drop in PM, etc.).

Ships with plugin Phase 3 (SDK gap-closure after polls). Not urgent for polls itself (already wired manually) but critical before PM + any third-party plugins — otherwise the translation-aware product becomes translation-aware-for-built-ins-only.

items.title: Validate polls federation across two-Tower staging
items.description:
Polls federation is wired on paper — the plugin ships a federationHandlers.poll entry, the plugin loader auto-mounts /plugins/polls/poll/by-slug/:slug, and the /embeds/resolve proxy dispatches cross-Tower refs to plugin kinds the same way it does first-party kinds. But none of it has been tested against a real second Tower.

Test plan (run on a two-Tower staging deploy, same rig as the voice / Firefox items):

1. Tower A user creates a poll. Shares the ref as [[poll:<slug>]] in a channel that federates to Tower B.
2. Tower B user (member of the federated channel) sees the embed card. Content renders via the signed-GET to A's by-slug endpoint through A's cross-Tower syntax: [[towerA@tower-a.example.com:poll:<slug>]].
3. Tower B user votes. Since v1 polls don't federate VOTES, expect a clean 401 / 403 from the vote endpoint. Document that limitation. (Voting-across-Towers is a follow-up that needs HTTP-signature-based auth on the vote call, same shape as the voice federation token pattern.)
4. Live vote-count sync on Tower A's side still broadcasts via Tower A's WS to its own users; Tower B users don't see live updates for cross-Tower embeds today. Document; track as a separate item if the need surfaces.
5. Federation policy test: Tower A sets FEDERATION_MODE=allowlist with Tower B absent. Tower B's /embeds/resolve fails with 403. Tower B adds to allowlist — works.

If Tower B users should be able to VOTE on Tower A's polls (likely yes for the shared-workspace pitch), that's the follow-up. The design mirrors the federated voice JWT handshake: home Tower signs a request to origin Tower for a vote-auth token; browser POSTs the vote with that token.

Blocked on the same infra as firefox-staging-voice-parity-test + live-federated-voice-end-to-end-test: needs two real Tower deployments with public DNS + TLS. Batch with those.

items.title: Plugin SDK — stable re-exports of @babelr/client utilities
items.description:
In-tree plugins today reach into client internals via relative paths like `../../client/src/hooks/useTranslateStrings.js`, `../../client/src/components/T.js`, and `../../client/src/plugins/ws-helper.js`. That's fine while plugins live in the monorepo but breaks the moment a plugin ships as an npm package (Phase 5).

Formalize stable re-exports so plugin authors import from a durable public surface:
- `@babelr/plugin-sdk/react` (or a separate `@babelr/plugin-ui` package) re-exports <T>, useTranslateStrings, ListDetailView, onWsMessage, and other primitives that polls + PM proved plugin authors want
- Semver-versioned: breaking changes bump the SDK major; plugins declare the range they need via dependencies.babelr-sdk
- Keeps React as a peer-dep on the SDK, not a direct dep — plugin authors bring their own React

Decision: subpath inside @babelr/plugin-sdk vs a separate @babelr/plugin-ui package. Subpath is simpler; separate package lets us update UI primitives independently of the manifest/runtime. Lean toward subpath until the split buys something concrete.

Ships with Phase 5 distribution freeze. Until then, the relative-path imports inside the monorepo are the documented pattern.

items.title: Plugin DB abstraction — helper over drizzle for plugin tables
items.description:
Plugins today write raw `sql\`...\`` template tags against fastify.db.execute(), with drizzle-orm as a direct dependency of each plugin package. This leaks the DB layer and couples plugins to a specific ORM version. If we swap drizzle out (or even bump a major version), every plugin breaks.

Possible abstractions:
- A plugin-scoped DB helper exposed on fastify via a plugin-sdk decorator: `fastify.pluginDb('polls').query('SELECT ...')` returns rows. Plugin never touches drizzle.
- A typed query builder scoped to the plugin's namespaced tables: `fastify.pluginDb.table('polls').where({ slug: ... }).first()`. Nicer DX, more to build.
- A migration DSL so plugins describe schemas declaratively rather than as raw SQL (closer to drizzle-kit's introspection but plugin-scoped).

Revisit after PM lands — we'll know what shapes of query plugins actually need and can design against real requirements instead of speculation. Polls' queries are simple enough that raw SQL works fine; PM's cross-table joins (work items with assignees + comments + sprints) may prove the raw-SQL pattern insufficient.

items.title: Plugin test harness — standard pattern for plugin unit + integration tests
items.description:
No story today for how plugin authors test their plugins. Polls has no tests. PM will be too large to ship without tests.

Scope:
- Server-side: a test helper that spins up a Fastify instance with the plugin registered, runs its migrations against a test DB, and injects HTTP requests. Pattern mirrors the existing `createTestApp` helper but scoped per-plugin.
- Client-side: vitest + React Testing Library with mocked translation settings and WS events. Plugin authors test their embed components and sidebar slots in isolation.
- Federation tests: reuse the createFederationTestApp pattern. Plugin-registered federation handlers get tested the same way first-party inbox handlers do.
- Example tests alongside each first-party plugin as reference implementations.

Ships alongside PM (Phase 4) when there's enough plugin code to need tests. The helper design emerges from what PM's tests actually want rather than being designed in isolation.

items.title: PM plugin federation — full end-to-end test on live federated Towers
items.description:
The project-management plugin ships a complete AS2 federation surface (outbound Create/Update/Delete of multi-typed ['Note','WorkItem'] objects, inbound inbox handlers, resolveBySlug for cross-Tower embeds, deliverToGroupFollowers decoration). None of this has been tested against a real second Tower — only against single-Tower dev and the type system.

Test plan (run on a two-Tower staging deploy, same rig as the voice / Firefox / polls-federation items):

1. Tower A user creates a board + work items. Verify Create activities appear in Tower B's inbox and produce shadow rows in plugin_pm_work_items. Tower B user sees them in the PM view.
2. Tower A user edits a work item (title, priority, description). Verify Update activity arrives at Tower B and the existing row patches. WS sync fires plugin:pm:updated on Tower B.
3. Tower A user deletes a work item. Verify Delete activity arrives at Tower B, row removed, chat OrderedCollection cleaned up.
4. Tower A user moves a card across columns (reorder). Reorder currently only broadcasts locally — remote Towers don't receive position-only changes. Verify no crash; document the limitation (cross-Tower reorder sync would need a new activity type or a bulk-position update). Track as follow-up if needed.
5. Cross-Tower embeds: Tower B user pastes [[towerA@tower-a.example.com:task:<slug>]] in a message. Verify TaskInline + TaskPreview render via signed-GET through the proxy. Same for [[board:<slug>]].
6. Comments: Tower B user opens a work item created on Tower A. Comment thread backed by the local chat_id OrderedCollection — comments are local to each Tower in v1. Document the limitation; cross-Tower comment threading is a follow-up that needs the Group-relay pattern extended to OrderedCollections within plugin objects.
7. Federation policy: Tower A sets FEDERATION_MODE=allowlist with Tower B absent. Verify inbound activities are rejected. Add Tower B — verify accepted.

Blocked on: two real Tower deployments with public DNS + TLS (same infra as live-federated-voice-end-to-end-test, firefox-staging-voice-parity-test, and polls-federation-live-test). Batch all four.

items.title: PWA validation on live Tower — install flow, offline shell, update cycle
items.description:
PWA ships with placeholder icons and has only been verified via the Vite build output (sw.js, manifest.webmanifest present, precache entries correct). Needs hands-on validation on a real deployed Tower with HTTPS:

1. Chrome desktop: install prompt appears, app opens in standalone window, icon on taskbar
2. Android Chrome: Add to Home Screen, splash screen, standalone mode, no URL bar
3. iOS Safari: Add to Home Screen via share sheet, apple-mobile-web-app-capable kicks in, black-translucent status bar, no Safari chrome
4. Offline: kill network, app shell loads from SW cache, messages show 'reconnecting' state, no browser error page
5. Update cycle: deploy a new version, revisit — autoUpdate SW installs silently, next page load picks up new assets
6. API caching: NetworkFirst with 5s timeout — verify stale API data shows briefly then refreshes when network returns

Blocked on same infra as the federation e2e tests: needs a real Tower with public DNS + TLS. Batch with those.

items.title: Babelr logo + PWA icons — generate candidates via ComfyUI
items.description: The PWA ships with 1x1 placeholder PNGs. Before any public-facing deployment, generate real icon artwork. Use ComfyUI to produce logo candidates — the name 'Babelr' + the Tower/translation/federation identity gives solid creative direction. Deliverables: favicon.svg, pwa-192.png, pwa-512.png (maskable-safe), apple-touch-icon, og:image for link previews. Consider the dark (#0a0a0a) background the app uses.

items.title: UX: migrate modal overlays to view registry for mobile-ready layout
items.description: 14 surfaces still use the old modal-overlay pattern from before the View/Sidebar paradigm was established. These don't work on mobile (no responsive layout, no back-gesture, no view transitions). Migrate all full-panel surfaces (settings, server settings, channel settings, friends, mentions, profile) to registered views. Merge profile into settings as a tab. Make MemberList the default right panel. Remove ChatView's show* state explosion. Sidebar auto-generates toolbar entries from the view registry.

items.title: UX: component primitives palette (TabbedView, ScrollListView, SidePanel)
items.description: Build a palette of reusable view primitives that both internal surfaces and plugin authors can leverage. TabbedView (tab bar + content panels), ScrollListView (scrollable list with header + search), SidePanel (right-panel overlay with close/escape). These sit alongside the existing ListDetailView. All get responsive CSS and dark-theme treatment. Exported from the plugin SDK as stable primitives.

items.title: Playwright integration test suite — full pass over all user-facing surfaces
items.description:
The 5 structural smoke tests prove Playwright works and catch container-type regressions. But we have zero coverage of actual user flows: creating a server, sending a message, voting on a poll, dragging a kanban card, opening embeds, voice join, federation cross-tower renders, translation toggling, 2FA setup, onboarding completion, etc.

Scope:
- Auth flows: register, login, 2FA challenge, email verification banner
- Onboarding: all 5 steps, skip paths, language detection
- Chat: send message, edit, delete, reactions, threads, embeds render inline, [[page:slug]] opens sidebar preview
- Servers: create, join, leave, invite links, server settings tabs
- Channels: create, edit settings, slow mode, glossary, private channels
- Voice: join/leave (verify UI state — can't test actual WebRTC in headless)
- Wiki: create page, edit, revisions, embed refs
- Calendar: create event, RSVP, event chat
- Files: upload, preview, download
- PM plugin: create board, create work item, drag-and-drop reorder, comments, embed preview
- Polls: create poll, vote, results update
- Translation: toggle provider, verify translated content appears
- Friends: send request, accept, DM from friend
- Members: role assignment, kick
- Settings: profile edit, password change, 2FA setup/disable
- PWA: manifest served, service worker registers (verify via JS eval)
- Responsive: narrow viewport renders mobile layout (once ux-8 ships)

Structure: one spec file per domain (auth.spec.ts, chat.spec.ts, wiki.spec.ts, etc.). Shared helpers for login, server creation, channel setup. Page object model if the suite grows past ~20 spec files.

Blocked on: nothing — can start anytime. Best batched after the UX views migration completes so we're testing the final layout, not an intermediate state.

items.title: Mobile UX refinement — navigation drawer, bottom tabs, sidebar content strategy
items.description:
The responsive breakpoints from the UX views migration prevent the app from being literally broken on mobile, but the experience is still 'desktop layout crammed into a small screen' rather than a mobile-native feel. Before going live, this needs a proper mobile-first treatment.

Areas to address:

1. **Navigation model**: The server sidebar + channel sidebar + chat panel + side panel four-column layout doesn't translate to mobile. Need a proper navigation stack or drawer model. Options: (a) hamburger drawer for server+channel sidebar that slides in from the left, (b) bottom tab bar for top-level nav (chat, calendar, wiki, settings), (c) swipe gestures between the navigation layers. Discord mobile uses a swipe-left-for-servers, swipe-right-for-members pattern — worth studying.

2. **Side panel content on mobile**: MemberList as always-visible default doesn't work on small screens. On mobile it should be behind a tap (member count badge in the header that opens a full-screen member list). Same for threads, glossary, and embed preview — all should be full-screen overlays with a back button on mobile rather than side panels.

3. **Bottom sheet pattern**: Modals (create server, create channel, new DM, channel invite) should use a bottom sheet on mobile instead of centered overlays — more thumb-friendly, standard mobile pattern.

4. **Touch targets**: Buttons, sidebar items, and interactive elements need minimum 44px touch targets on mobile. Current 0.4rem padding is too small.

5. **Keyboard handling**: Mobile keyboard pushes the message input off-screen on some devices. Need viewport-fit=cover + visual viewport API or position: sticky tricks.

6. **View transitions**: React view transitions or simple CSS animations for navigating between views. Currently the switch is instant, which feels jarring on mobile.

7. **Pull-to-refresh**: For message lists and board views.

This is a design-heavy effort — may want to prototype in Figma before coding. Consider doing a pass on a real phone (iPhone + Android) to identify the worst pain points before committing to an implementation approach.

Blocked on: nothing technically, but best done after the full Playwright integration suite so regressions are caught. The PWA live validation item should happen concurrently — testing on real devices surfaces issues no simulator catches.

items.title: Complexity refactor — split god files, extract shared patterns
items.description:
Theseus complexity analysis identified critical hotspots. Priority order:
1. Extract src/serializers.ts from channels.ts (50 lines, unblocks everything)
2. Split routes/channels.ts (1771 lines) into messages.ts + channel-settings.ts
3. Extract handleCreate dispatch table in inbox.ts (handler registry pattern)
4. Split useVoice.ts (1006 lines) into RPC/media/transport/peers hooks
5. Split PM plugin client.tsx (1212 lines) + manifest.ts (917 lines)
6. Quick wins: shared replyError helper (246 occurrences), centralized session-actor preHandler (28 occurrences)

items.title: Web push notifications — VAPID, service worker push, desktop Notification API
items.description: Users miss messages when the tab is backgrounded. Need web push (VAPID keys, subscription registration, server-side fan-out on new message/DM/mention) + desktop Notification API for foreground-but-unfocused. Badge count on the PWA icon. Respects per-channel mute + future DND settings. The PWA service worker already exists — extend it with push event handling.

items.title: Link preview / URL unfurling — og:image, title, description for pasted URLs
items.description: Every pasted URL renders as raw text. Server-side unfurler: on message create, detect URLs, fetch og:meta (title, description, image, site name), store as message properties. Client renders a compact preview card below the message. Rate-limited + cached to avoid abuse. Allowlist/blocklist for domains. Skip unfurling for embeds ([[kind:slug]]) since those have their own renderer.

items.title: Pinned messages — pin/unpin per channel with pinned-messages view
items.description: Basic channel organization. Schema: pinned_at timestamp + pinned_by on the message (or a junction table). Routes: POST /channels/:channelId/messages/:messageId/pin, DELETE to unpin, GET /channels/:channelId/pins. UI: pin icon in message context menu, 'Pinned' button in channel header opens a ScrollListView of pinned messages. Permission-gated (MANAGE_MESSAGES). Federate pin/unpin as AS2 Add/Remove activities.

items.title: User bans — permanent server ban with optional IP ban
items.description: Kick exists but banned users can rejoin immediately. Schema: server_bans(id, serverId, userId, bannedBy, reason, bannedAt, expiresAt). Routes: POST /servers/:serverId/ban (with optional duration for temp bans), DELETE to unban, GET to list bans. Join endpoint checks ban list before allowing membership. Permission: BAN_MEMBERS (new permission). Audit-logged. IP bans deferred (needs request IP tracking + privacy considerations).

items.title: User profile cards — view other users' profiles on click
items.description: Clicking a username or avatar in messages/member list should show a profile card with: avatar, display name, username, bio, roles in current server, member since date, mutual servers, and action buttons (DM, add friend, kick/ban for admins). Server-side: GET /users/:userId/profile returns public profile data. Client: ProfileCard component rendered in a popover or side panel.

items.title: Syntax highlighting for code blocks in messages
items.description: Code blocks in messages render without coloring. Add Shiki or highlight.js to the markdown renderer for fenced code blocks with language tags. The wiki enhancement item covers wiki content; this covers chat messages. Lazy-load the highlighter to avoid bloating the initial bundle.

items.title: Advanced search filters — from:user, in:channel, before/after:date, has:file
items.description: Full-text search exists but no filter syntax. Parse search queries for operator prefixes (from:, in:, before:, after:, has:) and add WHERE clauses to the existing PostgreSQL full-text query. UI: filter pills below the search bar showing active filters.

items.title: Do Not Disturb mode + quiet hours
items.description: Per-channel mute exists but no global DND. Add DND status to presence (online/away/dnd/offline). When DND: suppress all notifications and push, show a DND badge on avatar. Quiet hours: auto-enable DND between configured times (e.g. 10pm-8am). Stored on actor profile. Respected by push notification fan-out and desktop Notification API.

items.title: Jump to message — click notification/mention/search result to scroll to message in context
items.description: Currently clicking a mention or search result doesn't navigate to the message. Need: select the correct channel, load messages around the target message ID, scroll to it, and highlight it briefly. Server: GET /channels/:channelId/messages?around=messageId returns messages before and after. Client: scrollToMessage(channelId, messageId) function used by mentions view, search results, and push notification deep links.

items.title: Accessibility — ARIA landmarks, keyboard navigation, screen reader support, focus management
items.description: Minimal accessibility today (4 aria attributes on the PM plugin). Need: ARIA landmarks on major layout regions, keyboard navigation for all interactive elements, focus management on view transitions, skip-to-content link, high-contrast mode support, reduced-motion media query, screen reader announcements for live content (messages, notifications). Follow WCAG 2.1 AA. Test with VoiceOver + NVDA.

items.title: Video/audio/GIF inline players + media gallery
items.description: Image lightbox exists but no video/audio playback. Detect media type from file uploads and embeds. Video: inline HTML5 player with controls. Audio: compact waveform player. GIFs: auto-play inline (respect reduced-motion). Gallery view for scrolling through media in a channel. Lazy-load media to avoid bandwidth waste.

items.title: Server discovery browse UI — search, filter by tags, public server listing
items.description: The /servers/discover API returns public servers but there's no browse UI. Add a 'Discover Servers' view accessible from the server sidebar (+) button. Search by name, filter by tags, show member count + description. Join button. Mirrors Discord's server discovery but scoped to the local Tower (cross-Tower discovery is a federation follow-up).
